narrative: I'm sorry to deliver this news, but the law school's admission model predicted a 47.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was rejected—schools use this to prioritize students likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. You stand out with a strong LSAT score of 42, well above the typical student's 36.7, along with high family income and plans to attend full-time, which are common among applicants. However, your Year 1 school decile rank of 1—meaning you were in the bottom tenth of your class—is far below the average of about 5.7, and your Year 3 decile of 1 is similarly much lower than the typical 5.5. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.7 also trails the average of 3.21, while being male and White aligns closely with the majority of students in the dataset. When the model evaluated your profile, it weighed these factors to decide if you'd pass the bar, and the strongest influence pushing toward a failure prediction was your low Year 1 decile rank. Next in importance was your high family income, which also nudged the prediction toward failure, followed closely by your low Year 3 decile rank doing the same. Your undergraduate GPA ranked fourth in influence, again contributing toward the failure outlook, with your racial background as White fifth, your LSAT score sixth, full-time status seventh, and gender last—all subtly pushing in the failure direction to reach that 47.9% probability. Compared to typical students, your early and later school rankings and GPA are notable weak points that outweighed your LSAT strength in the model's eyes. This borderline prediction means you're not far off, but the low academic deciles signal potential challenges in sustaining performance through law school and the bar. To boost your future chances, focus on what you can control: building stronger study habits and academic consistency now, perhaps through targeted tutoring or coursework retakes if possible, since your school deciles and GPA were the top drivers and reflect patterns the model links to bar struggles. Improving in these areas could shift your profile toward the passing side for reapplication. With your solid LSAT as a foundation, you're already ahead there—keep leveraging it while addressing those early grade hurdles. Hang in there; many students turn things around with focused effort.
